title: DCM Workflow
text: vignette demonstrates new unified architecture running DCM model. Note DCM subtypes (HigherOrder, BayesNet, Traditional, MIRT, IRT) automatically determined.
code: # library(pgdcm)  # If running locally without installing: source("R/GraphBuilder.R") source("R/ModelConfig.R") source("R/Workflow.R")  # Helper to find data files whether installed or running locally get_data_path <- function(filename) {     path <- system.file("extdata", filename, package = "pgdcm")     if (path == "") path <- paste0("inst/extdata/", filename)     return(path) } print("Building Graph from Q-Matrix...") graph_res <- build_from_q_matrix(     QMatrixFile = get_data_path("Q_dummy.csv"),     DefaultAttributeCompute = "dina", # discrete DCM     DefaultTaskCompute = "dina" ) print("Creating DCM Model Configuration...")  DataDF <- read.csv(get_data_path("Data_dummy.csv"))  config <- build_model_config(graph_res, DataDF) print(paste("Configured Model Type:", config$type)) print("Starting Integrated Workflow...")  estimation_config <- list(     niter = 500,     nburnin = 100,     chains = 2,     prior_sims = 50,     post_sims = 50 )  results <- run_pgdcm_auto(     config = config,     estimation_config = estimation_config,     prefix = "Unified_DCM_Example" )

title: DCM Workflow

title: DCM Workflow
code: print("Building Graph from Q-Matrix...") graph_res <- build_from_q_matrix(     QMatrixFile = get_data_path("Q_dummy.csv"),     DefaultAttributeCompute = "dina", # discrete DCM     DefaultTaskCompute = "dina" )

code: print("Creating DCM Model Configuration...")  DataDF <- read.csv(get_data_path("Data_dummy.csv"))  config <- build_model_config(graph_res, DataDF) print(paste("Configured Model Type:", config$type))

code: print("Starting Integrated Workflow...")  estimation_config <- list(     niter = 500,     nburnin = 100,     chains = 2,     prior_sims = 50,     post_sims = 50 )  results <- run_pgdcm_auto(     config = config,     estimation_config = estimation_config,     prefix = "Unified_DCM_Example" )

title: SEM Workflow
text: vignette demonstrates new unified architecture running SEM model.
code: # library(pgdcm)  # If running locally without installing: source("R/GraphBuilder.R") source("R/ModelConfig.R") source("R/Workflow.R")  # Helper to find data files whether installed or running locally get_data_path <- function(filename) {     path <- system.file("extdata", filename, package = "pgdcm")     if (path == "") path <- paste0("inst/extdata/", filename)     return(path) } print("Building Graph from Files...") graph_res <- build_from_node_edge_files(     NodesFile = get_data_path("Znodes.csv"),     EdgesFile = get_data_path("Zedges.csv") ) print("Creating SEM Model Configuration...")  library(readxl) DataDF <- read_excel(get_data_path("Zdata.xlsx"))  config <- build_model_config(graph_res, DataDF) print(paste("Configured Model Type:", config$type)) print("Starting Integrated Workflow...")  estimation_config <- list(     niter = 2000,     nburnin = 500,     chains = 2,     prior_sims = 50,     post_sims = 50 )  results <- run_pgdcm_auto(     config = config,     estimation_config = estimation_config,     prefix = "Unified_SEM_Example" )

code: @Manual{,   title = {pgdcm: Probabilistic Graphical Diagnostic Classification Models},   author = {Athul Sudheesh and Richard M. Golden},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.1.0}, }

title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: new educational testing psychometrics, quickly encounter two major camps thought comes evaluating students: Item Response Theory (IRT): camp treats student’s ability Continuous Trait. Think spectrum like “General Intelligence” (θ\theta). student score anywhere -3.0 (struggling) +3.0 (gifted). higher score, higher smooth probability answering question correctly. Cognitive Diagnostic Models (CDMs): camp treats abilities Discrete Skills. Think checklist like “Knows Algebra” “Knows Geometry” (α\alpha). student either possesses skill (α=1\alpha = 1) (α=0\alpha = 0). Historically, software forced researchers choose: build continuous IRT model build discrete CDM model. real world messy. single advanced physics question requires general problem-solving intelligence (Continuous) specific memorized formula (Discrete)? pgdcm package solves using Bayesian Networks (BNs).

title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: Bayesian Network just mathematical map cause effect. web “nodes” (variables) connected arrows (dependencies). case, nodes Student Traits (like Intelligence Algebra skill) Test Questions (actual data observe). beauty Bayesian Network flexible. node doesn’t care parent node pointing continuous discrete. just takes inputs receives calculates final probability. document breaks exactly pgdcm package’s grading engine—custom function called calc_mixed_kernel inside loglinearBN.R script—handles mixing hood. reading guide, see almost every major psychometric model last 50 years can instantiated simple “special case” single Bayesian Network!

what: 2. The Core Mechanism: The Logistic Equation
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: diving scenarios, need understand Bayesian Network calculates probability. given test question (intermediate skill), model calculates probability student succeed using logistic equation: P(Success)≈logit−1(Slope×Effective Input−Intercept) P(\text{Success}) \approx \text{logit}^{-1}(\text{Slope} \times \text{Effective Input} - \text{Intercept}) Let’s translate plain English: Logistic Curve (logit−1\text{logit}^{-1}): just S-shaped curve squishes number valid probability 0% 100%. Slope (Discrimination): well question separate masters novices? steep slope means question highly sensitive student’s ability. Intercept (Difficulty): fundamentally difficult question? high intercept means need higher input score just get 50/50 chance passing. Effective Input (ψ\psi): magic number. calc_mixed_kernel calculates every single student every single question. looks prerequisites question requires, checks student possesses, spits single summary score representing “readiness.”

previous_headings: 2. The Core Mechanism: The Logistic Equation
what: 2.1 The Three Moving Parts of the Effective Input
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: calculate Effective Input, engine counts three things student: Continuous Sum (ScS_c): adds student’s continuous traits (like general intelligence) question requires . code, root nodes (variables nothing pointing ) treated continuous traits. Discrete Sum (SdS_d): counts many required discrete skills (like Algebra Geometry) student actually possesses. Required Mass (RdR_d): counts many discrete skills question formally demands. (e.g., question requires Algebra Geometry, Rd=2R_d = 2). engine three numbers, calculates Effective Input depending structure question.

what: 3. The Five Scenarios (How the BN Replicates Psychometrics)
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: , detail math plays five distinct real-world scenarios. see single Bayesian Network elegantly becomes different classic psychometric models simply changing arrows point nodes.

previous_headings: 3. The Five Scenarios (How the BN Replicates Psychometrics)
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: Imagine math-word problem requires “General Reading Ability” “General Math Ability”. require specific checklist skills. BN Engine Reads : discrete skills required, Required Mass (RdR_d) exactly 0. nothing discrete required, student automatically clears gate (0==00 == 0 true). Result: Effective Input (ψ\psi) simply sum continuous abilities: ScS_c (Math + Reading). MIRT: behaves exactly like Compensatory Multidimensional IRT (MIRT) model. Effective Input just adds continuous traits together (θmath+θreading\theta_{\text{math}} + \theta_{\text{reading}}), student high reading ability can mathematically “compensate” low math ability. combined sum still high enough push logistic curve.

previous_headings: 3. The Five Scenarios (How the BN Replicates Psychometrics)
what: Scenario 2: Standard Unidimensional IRT (Single Continuous Trait)
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: Imagine basic vocabulary question simply relies “General Reading Ability” nothing else. BN Engine Reads : Exactly like Scenario 1, one arrow pointing question. Result: Effective Input (ψ\psi) just single reading ability: ScS_c (Reading). 2PL IRT: behaves exactly like classic 2-Parameter Logistic (2PL) IRT model. Effective Input just student’s trait (θ\theta). higher trait, higher probability getting question right smooth curve, moderated question’s unique Slope Intercept.

previous_headings: 3. The Five Scenarios (How the BN Replicates Psychometrics)
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: Imagine strict math problem requires knowing exactly two rules: “Pythagorean Theorem” “Multiply Fractions”. General intelligence factor. BN Engine Reads : continuous traits involved (root nodes point question), Continuous Sum (ScS_c) internally turned . Required Mass (RdR_d) 2. engine strictly checks student’s Discrete Sum (SdS_d) equals 2. Result: student possesses skills (Sd=2S_d = 2), Effective Input 1. missing one (Sd<2S_d < 2), Effective Input 0. DINA Model: classic Deterministic Input, Noisy “” (DINA) Cognitive Diagnostic Model. probability success splits exactly two harsh groups regarding Effective Input: Masters (Input = 1): get high probability success (though 100%, allowing “slips”). Non-Masters (Input = 0): get low probability success (just baseline “guessing” chance). smooth curve ; step-function!

previous_headings: 3. The Five Scenarios (How the BN Replicates Psychometrics)
what: Scenario 4: The Gated Implementation (Mixed Continuous and Discrete)
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: breakthrough scenario unique framework. Imagine advanced physics question. requires high “General Problem Solving Ability” (Continuous θ\theta), absolutely must know “Newton’s Third Law” (Discrete α\alpha). model just added together (θ+α\theta + \alpha), pure genius student (θ=+3.0\theta = +3.0) doesn’t know Newton’s Law (α=0\alpha = 0) might still score high enough pass. physics strict; logic way knowing core formula! BN Engine Reads : prevent unfair compensation, engine treats discrete skills Strict Gate: compares student’s possessed discrete skills (SdS_d) requirement (RdR_d). pass Gate (Sd==RdS_d == R_d): gate opens! Effective Input becomes continuous intelligence (ScS_c). get use problem-solving ability scale logistic probability curve. fail Gate (Sd<RdS_d < R_d): engine intervenes. completely overrides continuous intelligence assigns extreme penalty: Effective Input = -10.0. Gated DINA: logistic math, Effective Input -10.0 translates ≈0.000045\approx 0.000045 probability success. drops straight zero. “gate”, extreme continuous intelligence compensate missing core knowledge. successfully creates Mixed-Domain Non-Compensatory (Gated DINA) model, solving hybridization problem!

previous_headings: 3. The Five Scenarios (How the BN Replicates Psychometrics)
what: Scenario 5: Compensatory and Disjunctive Topologies (DINM and DINO)
title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: researcher doesn’t want strict “--Nothing” rules DINA discrete skills? BN allows flipping flag use DINM DINO logic instead. DINM (Deterministic Input, Noisy “Multiplicative”): “Partial Credit” model. engine ignores strict “gate” entirely calculates ratio: (Possessed Inputs) / (Required Inputs). question requires 3 skills/traits student 2, Effective Input 2/32/3. High continuous ability can boost ratio , providing fully compensatory mixed model. DINO (Deterministic Input, Noisy “”): “Fast Track” model. engine asks one question: “student required skills traits > 0?” yes, Effective Input given full 1.0. single skill positive continuous trait enough succeed.

title: Unifying Psychometrics through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearBN Framework
text: viewing educational testing lens Bayesian Network, longer choose Continuous IRT models Discrete CDMs. calc_mixed_kernel function pgdcm package operates universal psychometric compiler. intelligently grouping continuous sums (ScS_c) discrete sums (SdS_d), applying strict gating mechanisms necessary, allows researchers build single Bayesian Network seamlessly switches standard 2PL IRT curves, compensatory MIRT planes, strict DINA categorizations, novel mixed-domain logic—evaluated concurrently node--node basis!

NULL, simulates priors. n_sim Numeric. Number simulations draw. Default 50. prefix Character. Prefix output plot filename. title Character. String used labeling (e.g., "PriorPPC").

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Structural Equation Modeling (SEM) powerful statistical technique used across social sciences map webs complex relationships. traditional testing models (like standard Item Response Theory), often assume simple structure: hidden trait (like “Math Ability”) directly causes student get specific math question right. world messier? knowing “Algebra” directly helps learn “Calculus”? (hidden trait causing another hidden trait). answering Question 1 correctly gives hint makes likely answer Question 2 correctly? (observed behavior causing another observed behavior). Standard psychometric tools often struggle interconnected feedback loops. pgdcm package handles massive, interconnected web instantiating SEM theories flexible Bayesian Network (BN) using loglinearSEM.R script. document breaks loglinearSEM mathematical engine works hood, using plain English explain gracefully calculates everything standard regressions complex network psychometrics.

what: 2. The Core Mechanism: The Linear Predictor
title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: fundamental philosophy loglinearSEM engine everything compensatory. runs engine classic linear regression. every node network (whether hidden student trait observed test score), model calculates overall score, mathematically known Linear Predictor: Overall Score=Intercept+(Weight1×Input1)+(Weight2×Input2)+… \text{Overall Score} = \text{Intercept} + (\text{Weight}_1 \times \text{Input}_1) + (\text{Weight}_2 \times \text{Input}_2) + \dots Let’s translate code variables loglinearSEM equation: Intercept (α\alpha / alpha): baseline value. student zero inputs (average inputs), natural baseline score probability? Weights (β\beta / beta): strength connection. highly skilled Algebra, exactly much boost Calculus score? high β\beta means strong causal relationship two variables. Inputs: actual current values parent nodes pointing node. math strictly additive, highly positive input one variable can historically “compensate” negative input another variable maintain high overall score.

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: makes loglinearSEM much powerful standard regression calculates overall score dynamically handling three distinct types relationships simultaneously within single causal network.

what: Type 1: Latent to Latent (Attribute to Attribute)
title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Example: “High Math Ability causes high Physics Ability.” model allows hidden traits map hierarchically. script loops latent attributes calculates linear_pred_att. Bayesian Network topologically sorted, calculates “root” traits first (like General Intelligence), naturally cascades downstream effects dependent traits (like Physics) evaluating .

what: Type 2: Latent to Observed (Attribute to Task)
title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Example: “High Physics Ability causes high score Question 1.” classic “Measurement Model” psychometrics. hidden traits point actual observable test questions. model calculates input_from_atts every specific question multiplying student’s hidden traits β\beta weights connecting specific question.

what: Type 3: Observed to Observed (Task to Task)
title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Example: “Solving Question 1 correctly gives student formula needed solve Question 2.” BNs truly shine. Traditional psychometric modeling assumes test questions completely independent factor ability (called “Local Independence”). BNs restriction! script calculates input_from_tasks. allows earlier test questions literally act weighted inputs later test questions, capturing “Network Psychometrics” test-taking momentum natively. Grand Total: test question, engine simply adds cascading logic together: Overall Score = Intercept + (Inputs Hidden Traits) + (Inputs Previous Questions)

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: far, calculated “Overall Score” (Linear Predictor). ? “Overall Score 2.1” GPA, 90% probability passing? SEM, variables can continuous (score 0 100) categorical (Pass/Fail). loglinearSEM code provides absolute flexibility letting researcher toggle “Flags” tell engine exactly distribute final score real-world statistics. checks flags Attributes (SEMdo...Attribute) Tasks (SEMdo...Task):

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Use Case: want traits scores strictly continuous, like standard GPAs, reaction times, survey scales. flag active, engine treats node Continuous Normal Distribution. takes Overall Score simply uses mean (μ\mu) bell curve. Node∼Normal(μ=Overall Score,σ=1) \text{Node} \sim \text{Normal}(\mu = \text{Overall Score}, \sigma = 1)  perfectly replicates classic Path Analysis standard continuous Structural Equation Modeling without transformation tricks.

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Use Case: want observed outcome strictly Pass/Fail (1 0). flag active, engine squishes Overall Score standard Logistic Curve. turns linear score spanning −∞-\infty +∞+\infty strict probability 0% 100%, flips weighted coin assign 1 0 based probability. P(Node=1)=logit−1(Overall Score) P(\text{Node} = 1) = \text{logit}^{-1}(\text{Overall Score})  replicates standard Logistic Regression inside network framework.

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: Use Case: want binary Pass/Fail outcome, want math align historically Normal Ogive parameters commonly used classic psychometrics. flag active, engine exactly thing Binary Model, actively multiplies Overall Score scaling factor 1.702 squishing . P(Node=1)=logit−1(1.702×Overall Score) P(\text{Node} = 1) = \text{logit}^{-1}(1.702 \times \text{Overall Score})  1.702? psychometrics, multiplying logistic score D≈1.702D \approx 1.702 scales logistic curve almost perfectly matches Cumulative Normal curve (Normal Ogive). famous mathematical trick allows modern software run fast Logistic equations perfectly replicating older Normal-based psychometric literature without losing accuracy.

title: Unifying Structural Equation Modeling through Bayesian Networks: The loglinearSEM Framework
text: stripping away confusing terminology different statistical packages, loglinearSEM script reveals unified truth: Structural Equation Modeling, Network Psychometrics, Path Analysis can unified one robust Bayesian architecture. calculating simple compensatory Linear Predictor (α+Σβx\alpha + \Sigma\beta x) providing toggle flags route predictor Continuous Bell Curve Binary Logistic Curve, single engine elegantly replicates decades distinct psychometric modeling techniques fewer 100 lines code.
